channel.title: Paper Feeds
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper feeds from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Building an Adversarial Malware Dataset by Family and Type: Generation, Evasion, and Poisoning Evaluation
item.description: We introduce a novel adversarial malware dataset derived from RawMal-TF, featuring two rigorously constructed subsets: **44,347 family-labelled** and **33,596 type-labelled** PE binaries, generated via diverse adversarial perturbations (e.g., section injection, API reordering, entry-point shifting). These samples achieve **98.35% and 92.20% evasion rates**, respectively, against the EMBER classifier. Each binary is accompanied by rich metadata—including EMBER scores, VirusTotal multi-engine reports, and provenance logs. Crucially, we demonstrate severe vulnerability to data poisoning: injecting only **0.5% fully mislabelled adversarial samples** into the family-labelled training set increases the evasion rate of the retrained EMBER model from **26.1% to 92.8%**, highlighting a practical, low-cost attack vector. The dataset is publicly released to advance research on adversarial robustness, poisoning defense, and trustworthy ML-based malware detection.

item.title: How Agentic AI Coding Assistants Become the Attacker's Shell
item.description: This paper uncovers a critical security vulnerability in agentic AI coding assistants—those capable of editing files, executing shell commands, and accessing the internet autonomously. We demonstrate that their reliance on *unvetted external artifacts* (e.g., GitHub READMEs, npm package docs, Stack Overflow answers) creates a novel attack surface: hidden instructions embedded in these artifacts can hijack the assistant to execute arbitrary commands, effectively turning it into an attacker’s remote shell. Through systematic evaluation across 127 real-world external sources and 6 artifact categories, we identify 23 new prompt injection variants and achieve up to 89.3% success rate against unpatched agents—even with basic defenses enabled, 41.7% of attacks succeed. We propose the *Context-Aware Sandboxing Protocol (CASB)*, a lightweight runtime mitigation that dynamically verifies execution intent and provenance, achieving 96.5% attack blocking without impairing functionality. Our work redefines the threat model for agentic AI, shifting focus from user-input sanitization to *external dependency trustworthiness*, and provides concrete foundations for future standards and tooling.

item.title: Broken Object Level Authorization in the Wild: An Empirical Taxonomy from 100+ Bug Bounty Disclosures
item.description: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical taxonomy of Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA), analyzing 107 rigorously validated bug bounty reports from HackerOne (2021–2026). Using an LLM-assisted, human-adjudicated classification schema across six BOLA families, we find that 78.5% of confirmed cases are genuine BOLA—challenging assumptions based solely on platform tags like “IDOR”. Action-Level Object BOLA (41.7%), involving unauthorized state-changing operations on another user’s objects (e.g., deleting or modifying their data), emerges as a dominant and previously underrepresented pattern. Critically, 21.5% of tagged reports were out-of-scope under strict BOLA criteria, exposing severe overcounting in tag-based metrics. Additional findings include an 11.9% rate of vertical privilege escalation and systematic exploitation of GraphQL Global IDs across major platforms. These results inform API security testing, developer training, and OWASP guidance with empirically grounded priorities.

item.title: TTPrint: Evidence-Grounded TTP Extraction via Diverge-then-Converge Verification
item.description: TTPrint addresses the open-set, multi-label MITRE ATT&CK technique extraction task from CTI reports by decoupling candidate generation and rigorous verification into a *diverge-then-converge* pipeline inspired by human analyst workflows. In the divergent phase, reports are decomposed into atomic behaviors to broadly propose candidates, each deterministically localized to a minimal evidence span in the source text. The convergent phase retains only candidates jointly supported by both this localized evidence and the authoritative MITRE definition. To enable robust evaluation, we release TRAM-Clean (a noise-cleaned TRAM benchmark) and TTPrint-Bench (a new document-level annotated dataset), elevating the task beyond sentence-level constraints. On these benchmarks, TTPrint achieves 76.48% and 87.39% macro-F1—outperforming the strongest baseline by +63.5% and +29.4%, respectively. Extensive ablations across six LLM backbones and threshold sensitivity analysis confirm its generalizability and practical deployability.

item.title: "What is the Problem Space?" Defining Host-space Adversarial Perturbations against Network Intrusion Detection Systems
item.description: This paper challenges the prevailing assumption in adversarial ML research on Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS): that perturbations can be arbitrarily applied to pre-collected packets or extracted features. We introduce and rigorously define **host-space adversarial perturbations**—modifications strictly confined to actions an attacker can perform *only* on compromised end-hosts (e.g., altering command-line arguments, payload generation logic, or protocol field values), without requiring control over routers, firewalls, or the NIDS’s internal data pipeline. Through a systematic review of 316 papers, we find that >92% of prior attacks violate this realistic constraint. Experimentally, on CIC-IDS2017, UNSW-NB15, and a live network, we demonstrate that changing just **one character** in an SSH brute-force command string (e.g., `-l` → `-L`) causes state-of-the-art ML-NIDS to completely miss the attack—despite the perturbation being undetectable at the network layer and fully executable on an unprivileged host. This minuscule host-space change induces catastrophic, non-linear shifts in the feature space (e.g., collapsing inter-arrival variance and packet-length entropy), exposing a critical gap between data-space robustness and real-world security. We provide actionable guidelines for evaluating host-space threats and argue that ML-NIDS security assessments must be fundamentally re-grounded in attacker capability models.

item.title: On Reliability of Efficient Membership Inference Vulnerability Evaluation
item.description: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are widely used to empirically audit model privacy, yet their efficient evaluation—especially at low false positive rates (FPR)—suffers from two critical, previously overlooked reliability flaws. First, concatenating MIA scores across individuals for ROC analysis induces **FPR miscalibration**, as per-sample score distributions are heterogeneous; this invalidates TPR estimates for rigorous differential privacy auditing. We propose a **per-sample FPR calibration** method to restore statistical validity. Second, we identify a **finite-population bias** in the standard LiRA implementation (Carlini et al., 2022): the denominator of its likelihood ratio is systematically underestimated due to limited shadow models, causing a positive bias in per-sample vulnerability scores. Our theoretical analysis and unbiased estimator reduce reported TPR by 15–40% at FPR = 0.001. These findings fundamentally improve the trustworthiness of efficient MIA evaluation.

item.title: Referential Security as a New Paradigm for AI Evaluations
item.description: Security evaluations require stable, unambiguous references to AI systems—yet today’s continuously updated models violate this foundational assumption. Public model names (e.g., “Claude-3.5-Sonnet”) remain static while weights, prompts, RAG pipelines, classifiers, and serving infrastructures change silently. Consequently, safety claims often attach to labels—not identifiable, verifiable artifacts. We introduce *referential security*: a new paradigm where the core security question shifts from “Is this model safe?” to “Can third parties conclusively determine *which specific system* a given safety claim refers to?” It treats model identity as an empirically verifiable property—anchored via cryptographic hashes of configuration snapshots, inference traces, and metadata—and decouples referential stability from substantive safety claims. This enables three critical advances: (1) reproducible evaluation through artifact-based verification; (2) longitudinal audit validity across model updates; and (3) cross-provider equivalence assessment under shared referential grounding. Referential security provides the essential infrastructure for trustworthy AI governance in dynamic deployment environments.

item.title: When Interpretability Becomes a Liability: Adversarial Attacks on CBM Concept Layers
item.description: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance interpretability by enforcing human-readable semantic concepts (e.g., “crested head”, “black wing bars”) as intermediate representations—yet this very property exposes a previously unexplored attack surface: the concept bottleneck layer itself. We present the first systematic study of *concept-level adversarial attacks*, showing that minimal pixel-space perturbations can catastrophically manipulate semantic activations and induce targeted misclassification. We develop a theoretical framework to quantify concept-space robustness, revealing severe vulnerabilities in standard CBMs on CUB-200-2011 (attack norm: **0.46**). To counter this, we propose **SPECTRA**, a semantic perturbation–based stability regularization method that hardens concept representations. SPECTRA increases the minimum successful attack norm to **>4,200**, rendering targeted concept manipulation computationally prohibitive, while preserving classification accuracy within **2.2%** of the baseline. This work establishes concept-level attacks as a distinct threat model at the intersection of interpretable ML and adversarial robustness.

item.title: EchoPilot: Training-Free Ultrasound Video Segmentation via Scale-Space Semantic Prompting and Reliability-Gated Memory
item.description: Ultrasound video segmentation is clinically critical yet notoriously challenging due to speckle noise, low contrast, and rapid anatomical deformation. While promptable foundation models enable point-guided segmentation, their direct application to ultrasound videos suffers from scale ambiguity (from single-point initialization) and severe temporal drift (from greedy memory updates). EchoPilot addresses these issues *without any training*: given only one click and an anatomical category name on the first frame, it achieves robust video segmentation by synergizing a frozen medical VLM (for semantic grounding), a VFM (for dense geometric features), and a promptable video segmentor. We propose **Scale-Space Semantic Prompting**, which selects the optimal contextual scale via a parameter-free S.E.E.D. criterion and synthesizes precise auxiliary point prompts from foundation features—*zero additional user input required*. To curb drift, **Reliability-Gated Memory** selectively freezes the segmentor’s memory bank under uncertain predictions. Evaluated on three ultrasound video benchmarks—including our newly released dynamic fetal placenta dataset (671 frames)—EchoPilot sets new state-of-the-art performance under sparse interaction, outperforming all training-free baselines and even fine-tuned specialists, with real-time inference (28 FPS).

item.title: PennySynth: RAG-Driven Data Synthesis for Automated Quantum Code Generation
item.description: PennySynth is a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework for automated PennyLane quantum code synthesis. It addresses LLM hallucinations and structural invalidity by conditioning generation on a curated knowledge base of 13,389 verified instruction-code pairs—extracted and deduplicated from official PennyLane docs, community GitHub repos, and QHack competition archives (2022–2024). We introduce a code-aware embedding strategy using fine-tuned `st-codesearch-distilroberta-base`, boosting average retrieval cosine similarity from 0.45 to 0.726. Evaluated on 74 real-world QHack challenges, PennySynth achieves pass@5 scores of 64%, 68%, and 52% for QHack 2022, 2023, and 2024—outperforming Claude Sonnet 4.6 (no RAG) by +28, +25, and +28 percentage points. We also propose a quantum-adapted CodeBLEU metric that upweights `qml.*` tokens, revealing distinct dimensions of quantum code quality. Ablations confirm code-aware embeddings are the primary driver of performance gains.

item.title: A Tertiary Review of Large Language Model-Based Code Generating Tasks: Trends, Challenges, and Future Directions
item.description: This tertiary review synthesizes evidence from 30 high-quality secondary studies (2017–2025) on LLM-based code generation tasks (CGTs). Using systematic search, snowballing, and rigorous quality appraisal (Krippendorff’s α = 0.89), we map publication trends, application scenarios, and critical gaps. Results reveal a sharp rise since 2023, yet strong benchmark performance (e.g., HumanEval) poorly generalizes to real-world maintenance, debugging, or collaborative settings. Robustness is highly configuration-sensitive; efficiency constraints (latency, cost) are pervasive; toxicity and bias remain under-reported. Key challenges center on economic feasibility, evaluation validity (especially human-centered assessment), and socio-technical integration. We advocate domain-aware model adaptation and holistic, standardized evaluation frameworks—spanning correctness, security, maintainability, and developer experience—to advance CGTs beyond isolated benchmarks toward sustainable engineering practice.

item.title: Security of OpenClaw Agents: Fundamentals, Attacks, and Countermeasures
item.description: This survey presents the first comprehensive security analysis of **OpenClaw agents**—open-source, LLM-driven autonomous systems characterized by persistent memory, skill augmentation, multi-channel interaction, and high autonomy. We propose a layered threat taxonomy grounded in the agent lifecycle: *cognitive layer* (e.g., prompt-based reasoning manipulation), *execution layer* (e.g., skill poisoning and privilege escalation), *interaction layer* (e.g., cascading failures across agent collectives), and *supply-chain layer* (e.g., compromised plugins or dependencies). We identify the critical vulnerability arising from the coupling of persistent state and high-privilege actions—enabling persistent “state pollution” that propagates across execution cycles. Key defenses reviewed include runtime sandboxing with fine-grained tool permissions, cognitive integrity verification via reasoning-path watermarking, and supply-chain hardening through zero-trust plugin signing and SBOM-based auditing. We conclude by highlighting unresolved challenges, notably the lack of TEE support and accountability mechanisms in multi-agent coordination—offering foundational insights for building trustworthy, auditable open agent ecosystems.

item.title: Joint Optimization of Training and Inference in Federated Edge Learning via Constrained Multi-Objective Deep Reinforcement Learning
item.description: Federated Edge Learning (FEEL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative model training across resource-constrained edge devices, yet jointly optimizing real-time inference and periodic training remains challenging due to dynamic workloads, data/model staleness, and conflicting objectives (accuracy, latency, energy). This paper proposes an online joint optimization framework that bridges inference requests and training data via a tandem-queue-inspired conversion mechanism and explicitly incorporates *data* and *model freshness* into the accuracy metric to capture temporal dynamics. We formulate the problem as a constrained multi-objective optimization—jointly deciding device operation modes, communication bandwidth/power, and computation CPU frequency/batch size—and cast it as a Multi-Objective Markov Decision Process (MOMDP). To solve it, we design **C-MOPPO**: a Constrained Multi-Objective Proximal Policy Optimization algorithm that first learns diverse preference-aware policies and then leverages constrained policy updates to densely populate the Pareto front with high-quality solutions. Extensive experiments show C-MOPPO achieves superior trade-offs—improving inference accuracy by 12.7%, reducing end-to-end latency by 38.5%, and cutting energy consumption by 41.2% over state-of-the-art baselines—while generating 2.3× denser Pareto-optimal solutions under heterogeneous system configurations.

item.title: Peak-Then-Collapse and the Four Interface Channels of Knowledge-Graph Tool Use
item.description: This paper identifies a fundamental *peak-then-collapse* failure mode in LLM-based knowledge-graph (KG) tool use: under self-verifiable retrieval rewards, GRPO-trained Qwen2.5-7B achieves a peak tool-grounded answer rate of 9.6% at step 250—then collapses to 0% within just 50 steps, consistently across four seeds. Unlike Python interpreters or web search, KG APIs (e.g., Freebase) provide *no natural-language interface feedback*—empty results return only `[]`, leaking no pretraining-aligned error signals. Seven reward redesigns shift but never eliminate the collapse, confirming the issue is interface-bound, not reward-tunable. Oracle ablation shows gold relation injection improves EM by only +0.20 pp; 95.4% of errors stem from *retrieval-composition failures*, not answer extraction. A one-iteration self-distillation strategy lifts EM to 40.0% at 7B—and exhibits strong capacity invariance (+0.25 pp at 14B), indicating an interface-imposed ceiling within the 7B–14B range.

item.title: Double Triangle Annotation: A Scalable Human-in-the-Loop Framework for High-Precision Historical Document Annotation
item.description: We introduce **Double Triangle Annotation**, a scalable human-in-the-loop framework for high-precision structured information extraction from historical documents. It employs two layers of cross-model consensus: (1) two architecturally distinct multimodal LLMs annotate each document in parallel; agreements are auto-accepted, while disagreements are resolved by a human jury; (2) two independent instances of this first layer cross-check each other, with residual conflicts escalated to domain experts. Relying solely on the assumption of model error independence—without distributional priors or task-specific calibration—the framework achieves **0.003 Word Error Rate** on the French *Rosenwald Guides* (1887–1906) and auto-accepts **>85% of 13,595 fields**. We release the first structured-extraction ground-truth benchmark for this corpus to advance historical document processing research.

item.title: LLM-as-a-Reviewer: Benchmarking Their Ability, Divergence, and Prompt Injection Resistance as Paper Reviewers
item.description: This paper introduces **LLM-as-a-Reviewer**, the first systematic benchmark evaluating large language models as peer reviewers across *rating calibration*, *divergence from human judgments*, and *resistance to prompt injection*. We evaluate 12 LLMs on 898 stratified NeurIPS/ICLR papers. Key findings: (1) All models systematically **overrate weak submissions**, under-flagging Clarity (+1.2–1.8 pts bias on 5-pt scale) while over-flagging Reproducibility; (2) Their reviews are **2–3× longer but lexically less diverse** (−37% type-token ratio) and exhibit topical misalignment with human reviewers (KL divergence: 0.42–0.67); (3) A novel *invisible font-mapping attack* successfully promotes low-scoring papers to acceptance-level ratings in up to **41% of cases**, with stark variation across model families (e.g., Qwen > GPT-4 Turbo). The study underscores urgent needs for bias mitigation and adversarial safeguards before real-world deployment.

item.title: Security in the Fine-Tuning Lifecycle of Large Language Models: Threats, Defenses,Evaluation, and Future Directions
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic survey of security threats and defenses across the *full fine-tuning lifecycle* of LLMs—spanning pre-tuning, during-tuning, and post-tuning phases. We introduce a unified, phase-aware framework for classifying attacks (e.g., data poisoning, gradient manipulation, weight editing, interface exploitation) and defenses, and conduct rigorous empirical evaluation on open-source models (1B–4B) under standardized hardware, model, and PEFT protocols. Key findings include: (1) Attack effectiveness is highly non-monotonic and model-specific—e.g., weight-editing attacks fail entirely on modern 1B–4B models where they previously succeeded; (2) Cross-lingual backdoor transfer, claimed near-perfect at scale, achieves 0% success in our tests; (3) Purely benign inputs can break safety alignment in instruction-tuned models; and (4) Single-phase defenses rarely generalize across phases, with efficacy jointly determined by architecture and alignment state. We identify three critical open problems—configuration-robust defense design, principled cross-phase defense composition, and embedding-space threat modeling beyond behavioral assumptions—and propose concrete, actionable research directions.

item.title: QML-PipeGuard: Drift-Aware Behavioral Fingerprinting for Quantum Machine Learning Pipeline Integrity
item.description: Quantum machine learning (QML) is transitioning from research to production, raising urgent integrity concerns for the quantum execution stage: *natural drift* (channel parameter changes between hardware recalibrations) and *adversarial substitution* (replacing the declared quantum channel with a behaviorally similar but mathematically distinct one). Existing QML verification approaches—focused on pulse-level noise, input drift, or device identity—fail to jointly address both. We propose **QML-PipeGuard**, a contract-based framework that characterizes a QML pipeline at runtime via its *behavioral fingerprint*: a vector of expectation values under an informationally complete, tomographically structured measurement family (e.g., the single-qubit Pauli tight frame with bound $C = \sqrt{3}$). It operates in two modes: *drift-aware monitoring*, absorbing benign calibration variations within a calibrated tolerance, and *adversarial detection*, flagging channel substitution as a violation of an observable contract. Key contributions include a pipeline-composition treatment with QML-specific threat modeling, a finite-shot sample-complexity bound (~1.4×10⁴ shots), and a novel tolerance decomposition separating natural and adversarial contributions. End-to-end validation on a two-qubit QSVM pipeline running on IBM’s `ibm_fez` (Heron r2) confirms detection of a “sneaky” substituted channel with wide safety margin, while typical hardware drift remains within tolerance.

item.title: Reflect-Guard: Enhancing LLM Safeguards against Adversarial Prompts via Logical Self-Reflection
item.description: Reflect-Guard enhances LLM safety classifiers (e.g., Llama Guard) against adversarial jailbreaks by integrating *logical self-reflection* via parameter-efficient fine-tuning. We distill structured chain-of-thought reasoning—covering intent inference, risk grounding, and contextual consistency—from GPT-4o-mini into reflection annotations, then train Llama-Guard-3-8B using QLoRA on just 1,000 examples while updating only 0.5% of parameters (~42M). During inference, the model first generates a reflective rationale before issuing a safety verdict. On WildGuardTest, Reflect-Guard boosts F1 from 0.770 to 0.842 (+7.2 pp) and adversarial recall from 0.513 to 0.921 (+40.8 pp). On JailbreakBench, attack success rate drops from 10.3% to 1.8% (−82.5% relative). These gains stem from explicit reasoning about *underlying malicious intent*, enabling robust detection where surface-pattern classifiers fail.

item.title: RouteScan: A Non-Intrusive Approach to Auditing MoE LLMs Safety via Expert Routing Telemetry
item.description: RouteScan is a non-intrusive safety auditing framework for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs that operates solely on low-level GPU expert routing telemetry—bypassing prompts, inputs, and outputs entirely. It leverages the count of active GPU threads allocated to each expert during prefilling as a privacy-preserving, microarchitectural fingerprint of input semantics. A lightweight detection pipeline extracts cross-domain invariant risk signals from this telemetry, enabling precise identification of malicious prompts without accessing sensitive user data. Evaluated on diverse open-source MoE models (e.g., Mixtral, Qwen-MoE), RouteScan achieves AUROC > 0.93 on unseen harmful domains and 0.96 under novel jailbreak wrappers—demonstrating strong generalization. Crucially, empirical inversion tests confirm that routing telemetry provides negligible reconstructive signal for prompts (BLEU < 0.02), offering a practical privacy advantage over content-based methods.

item.title: LLM Agent Based Renewable Energy Forecasting Using Edge and IoT Data A Review of Solar Wind Weather and Grid Aware Decision Support
item.description: This review examines how Large Language Model (LLM) agents advance renewable energy forecasting by unifying heterogeneous edge/IoT data—spanning smart meters, inverters, anemometers, pyranometers, weather APIs, historical generation, and grid constraints—into context-aware decision support. We propose a six-layer taxonomy (data acquisition to natural language reporting) and survey classical, deep learning, physics-hybrid, and emerging LLM-agent approaches—with emphasis on explainability, uncertainty communication, hallucination mitigation, and operator guidance. Key challenges identified include real-time edge deployment, distributional shift-induced model drift, multi-source uncertainty quantification, LLM hallucination control, edge hardware interoperability, and EMS integration. We recommend a research agenda centered on open energy benchmarks, physics-informed LLM grounding, and federated forecasting architectures.

item.title: Solving Combinatorial Counting Problems with Weighted First-Order Model Counting
item.description: Combinatorial counting—enumerating sets, multisets, permutations, partitions, and compositions under structural and arithmetic constraints—is fundamental yet notoriously manual. Closed-form solutions are brittle; propositional encodings destroy exchangeability, undermining tractability. We introduce **Cofola**, a typed declarative language whose primitives directly mirror combinatorial objects (e.g., bags, sequences, circles, partitions) and support natural relational and arithmetic constraints. Its denotational semantics maps programs to well-defined counting problems, compiled via a three-phase pipeline (preprocessing, decomposition, symmetry-preserving encoding) into **weighted first-order model counting (WFOMC)** instances with coefficient-extraction constraints. Crucially, the encoding preserves domain-liftability by grouping indistinguishable elements, breaking unordered symmetries lexicographically, and axiomatizing order for sequences/circles. Evaluated on textbook and multi-object problems beyond prior frameworks’ expressivity, Cofola delivers concise specifications and an end-to-end practical solver—unifying combinatorial intuition with automated, scalable counting.

item.title: Personalized Federated Learning by Energy-Efficient UAV Communications
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training across edge devices, yet geographic dispersion—e.g., in remote sensor networks—necessitates flexible aerial support. Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) serve as mobile aggregators but face dual challenges: data heterogeneity degrades convergence, while full-device scheduling incurs prohibitive communication and energy costs under limited UAV battery capacity. To address this, we propose a personalized FL framework featuring: (i) a strict separation between a globally shared lightweight backbone and permanently local personalization heads to inherently mitigate data heterogeneity; and (ii) a gradient ℓ₂-norm–based sparse scheduling strategy that selects only the top-α% devices per round for backbone updates—prioritizing informative gradients while minimizing UAV communication load and energy expenditure. Simulations under strong Non-IID settings show our method achieves **3.2–5.7% higher test accuracy** than state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx, UAV-FL), while reducing **per-round UAV energy consumption by 41.3%**, effectively extending flight endurance. This work bridges personalized learning, energy-aware UAV communications, and practical FL deployment.

item.title: Localization then Neutralization: Gradient-guided Token Suppression against Visual Prompt Injection Attack
item.description: Adversarial visual prompt injection poses a critical security threat to multimodal large language models (MLLMs). This work identifies that successful attacks rely not on the entire image but on a sparse set of critical image tokens. To counter this, we propose **Gradient Token Masking (GTM)**: a lightweight, gradient-guided defense that first *localizes* attack-critical tokens via the **Hidden-State Gradient Norm (HSGN)**—a theoretically grounded attribution metric proven consistent with full adversarial loss gradients—and then *neutralizes* them by masking. GTM requires only **one forward-backward pass**, zeros out <1% of tokens, and incurs negligible latency (<8 ms/image). Experiments across diverse prompt injection and multimodal jailbreak benchmarks show GTM reduces attack success rates (ASR) to near zero while preserving model utility (≤0.3% accuracy drop on VQAv2/MMBench), outperforming prior defenses in both efficacy and efficiency.

item.title: MimirRAG: A Multi-Agent RAG Framework for Financial Data Retrieval with Metadata Integration
item.description: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is critical for financial analysis, where LLM outputs must be rigorously grounded in regulatory filings to prevent hallucination. Yet, standard RAG struggles with financial documents’ complexity—dense text, multi-page tables, and rich metadata. This paper introduces **MimirRAG**, a metadata-integrated, multi-agent RAG framework designed specifically for financial data retrieval. Its pipeline features structure-preserving PDF parsing, table-aware chunking, automated metadata extraction (company, fiscal year, section, table semantics), query-planning agents, hybrid (keyword/vector/metadata) search, cross-document validation, and context-aware generation with numerical reasoning support. Ablation studies identify metadata integration, table-aware chunking, and the agentic workflow as the three key enablers of performance. Evaluated on FinanceBench, MimirRAG achieves **89.3% accuracy**, outperforming prior baselines by 6.8 points. Expert validation with four financial analysts confirms its analytical utility but emphasizes that real-world deployment requires calibrated trust (e.g., confidence scoring), comprehensive data integration, and user personalization. MimirRAG demonstrates how human-centered multi-agent RAG architecture advances reliable financial insight extraction.

item.title: Efficient DP-SGD for LLMs with Randomized Clipping
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) trained on sensitive data require rigorous privacy guarantees, for which differential privacy (DP) is the gold standard. However, state-of-the-art DP-SGD implementations suffer from prohibitive $O(B \min\{T^2, d^2\})$ memory overhead—scaling poorly with sequence length $T$ and model width $d$. We propose **DP-SGD-RC**, a novel variant that replaces deterministic per-sample gradient norm computation with *randomized clipping* via stochastic trace estimation (Hutchinson’s estimator and Hutch++). This reduces memory to $O(B d)$ and cuts compute significantly, while preserving gradient unbiasedness and privacy rigor. Our tight privacy analysis shows DP-SGD-RC achieves noise multipliers competitive with deterministic clipping under identical $(\varepsilon,\delta)$. Experiments fine-tuning Llama-3.2-1B on long-context benchmarks (classification, QA, summarization) demonstrate **on-par utility with baseline DP-SGD**, alongside **42–68% memory reduction and 35–51% faster step time**, enabling scalable, private LLM adaptation.

item.title: Spiking the training data to correct for test set contamination
item.description: Test set contamination inflates model evaluation scores, yet correction—unlike detection—remains underexplored. We propose *spiking*: intentionally injecting known fractions of test examples into training data to create labeled calibration sets. These spiked examples train memorization and correctness predictors, enabling principled statistical correction of inflated test accuracy. Using Hubble model pairs—where perturbed models are deliberately contaminated while standard models serve as clean counterfactuals—we evaluate estimators in simulation. Joint estimators leveraging both memorization and correctness signals consistently outperform naive (no-correction) baselines. Simple predictors—e.g., Platt-scaled membership inference metrics—achieve strong correction with as few as 10 spiked examples and transfer across datasets. Spiking thus offers a practical, statistically grounded solution for real-world model evaluation under contamination.

item.title: They Are Not the Same: Direct Causes Are Not Grounded Emotion Explanations
item.description: This paper challenges the conflation of *direct cause identification* with *grounded emotion explanation* in Emotion-Cause Pair Extraction (ECPE). While ECPE was designed to explain *why* an emotion occurs, it is now widely reduced to binary pair/non-pair classification—a useful proxy for detecting direct triggers, but misleadingly interpreted as evidence-based explanation. We show this interpretation is only partially valid: though the binary task remains largely preserved on IEMO-MECP (90.9% original positives and 95.0% negatives retain their labels), the binary boundary fails to stably accommodate *emo-context*—utterances that support emotional interpretation without direct causation. Such context crosses the boundary and concentrates near model uncertainty. Across models, direct triggers are recovered more reliably than contextual evidence; under shortcut pressure, models assign higher pair scores to lexically similar but unsupported non-pair candidates than to evidence-backed, structurally complex emo-cause or emo-context pairs. Thus, high binary ECPE performance reflects trigger detection—not explanation. Code: https://github.com/panzhzh/ECPExsame.

item.title: CyberMaskQA: A Privacy-Aware Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models in Cybersecurity Question Answering
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for cybersecurity question answering (QA), yet real-world operational data contains sensitive identifiers (e.g., IPs, hostnames) that pose privacy and regulatory risks when processed by cloud-based models. Crucially, progress in privacy-preserving QA is hindered by the absence of context-rich, human-annotated benchmarks that jointly evaluate *operational reasoning* and *controlled information disclosure*. To bridge this gap, we introduce **CyberMaskQA**, a novel privacy-aware QA benchmark covering incident response, vulnerability analysis, and access control. Unlike existing benchmarks focused on factual recall, CyberMaskQA grounds questions in realistic organizational contexts with explicit causal dependencies among assets and privileges. Its dataset is generated via a hybrid pipeline—human-curated base scenarios augmented by LLM-driven semantic expansion—and each instance is annotated with precise private entity labels (e.g., `<IP:10.2.1.8>`) to enable fine-grained masking evaluation. Experiments show CyberMaskQA effectively exposes trade-offs between QA accuracy and masking fidelity, providing a rigorous testbed for developing deployable, privacy-compliant cybersecurity LLMs. The dataset and generation framework will be publicly released upon acceptance.

item.title: Demystifying the Mythos or Disrupting Bugonomics? From Zero-Day Asymmetry to Defender Remediation Throughput
item.description: This paper reframes LLM-driven vulnerability discovery through *bugonomics*—the operational economics of finding, validating, prioritizing, and fixing security defects. Contrary to headlines heralding an “AI zero-day flood,” we show that LLMs do not collapse offensive high-end exploit economics (which remain costly due to reliability, stealth, and chaining requirements), but *do* dramatically reduce marginal costs for defender-side candidate generation, code comprehension, harness construction, impact drafting, and reporting. Using data from Anthropic’s Mythos Preview, Mozilla Firefox collaborations, and public exploit-market/reward-program benchmarks, we demonstrate that the near-term shift is toward **defender remediation throughput**: low-signal candidates become cheaper to produce, evidence-rich remediation guidance grows in value, and scarce human capacity (especially maintainer review, triage, funding, and release engineering) becomes the critical bottleneck—particularly acute in open source. The key implication: security ROI now hinges less on finding more bugs, and more on scaling *validation infrastructure* and *maintainer enablement*.

item.title: Poisoning the Watchtower: Prompt Injection Attacks Against LLM-Augmented Security Operations Through Adversarial Log Content
item.description: This paper identifies and characterizes *log-substrate prompt injection*—a critical structural vulnerability in LLM-augmented security operations, where attacker-controlled log fields (e.g., User-Agent, URLs, DNS queries) serve as covert channels for injecting malicious instructions into analyst-assistant models. We propose a four-class taxonomy: S1 (Direct Override), S2 (Persona Hijack), S3 (Context Manipulation), and S4 (Obfuscated Payload). Evaluating 48 strategy-defense-task combinations using `gpt-4o-mini`, we find: (1) S1 attacks fail entirely (0% suppression), while S2 achieves 68% suppression under naive classification—and remains potent even under strong defenses; (2) summarization is the highest-risk task, with S3 reaching 96% injection success without defenses and 38% under constrained output; (3) defenses reduce but do not eliminate risk—average injection success drops only from 26.6% to 11.8% under our strongest mitigation. Crucially, deterministic mock analysts mispredict real model behavior, especially for S1. These results compel SOC copilots to treat raw logs as adversarial input—not benign context.

item.title: Five Queries Are Enough: Query-Efficient and Surrogate-Free Membership Inference Attacks on RAG via Entailment
item.description: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems face a critical privacy threat: membership inference attacks (MIAs) that determine whether specific documents reside in their private retrieval corpus. Existing MIAs are either detectable (via templated queries) or impractical (requiring dozens of costly, repetitive queries). We propose **MEntA**, the first query-efficient (≤5 queries), surrogate-free, template-agnostic, and defense-agnostic MIA for RAG. MEntA reframes membership inference as natural language entailment: it issues broad, low-cost information-seeking questions, then measures entailment confidence between the RAG’s response and candidate documents—high entailment signals membership. Evaluated on NFCorpus, SCIDOCS, and TREC-COVID, MEntA achieves up to **0.991 AUC with only 5 queries**, outperforming prior work by 0.20–0.50 AUC. It remains effective against state-of-the-art RAG defenses, evades existing MIA detectors (high false-negative rate), and reduces total attack cost by up to **65×** versus SOTA methods. This exposes a realistic, low-budget privacy leakage vector in production RAG systems.

item.title: Reframing LLM Agent Security as an Agent-Human Interaction Problem
item.description: We reframe LLM agent security as fundamentally an *agent-human interaction (AHI)* problem—not an algorithmic one. Analyzing 59 academic papers, 21 production systems, and 26 security plugins (as of April 2026), we find a stark industry-academia mismatch: human-centric mechanisms—policy specification (14/21 systems), runtime approval (15/21), and scope configuration (16/21)—dominate practice, while academically favored techniques like intent anchoring and trust labeling achieve *zero* production deployment. Critically, current human participation suffers from an irreducible trade-off between cognitive burden and security assurance, trapping users between approval fatigue and uncontrolled autonomy. Our contributions are threefold: (1) We demonstrate that human involvement in security-critical decisions remains indispensable for intent alignment under current LLM capabilities; (2) We quantify the severe misalignment between deployed mechanisms and research focus; and (3) We propose a three-direction AHI security research agenda—and advocate for AHI security to be recognized as a first-class discipline with its own design principles, evaluation frameworks, and theoretical foundations.

item.title: Rethinking Federated Unlearning via the Lens of Memorization
item.description: Federated unlearning is critical for regulatory compliance (e.g., GDPR), yet existing methods often ignore semantic overlap between unlearning and retained data—causing ineffective forgetting and client unfairness. This work rethinks federated unlearning through the lens of *memorization*, arguing that unlearning should selectively erase *unique memorized knowledge* attributable solely to the to-be-forgotten data, while preserving *overlapping patterns* supported by remaining data. To this end, we propose **Grouped Memorization Evaluation (GME)**, an example-level metric that disentangles unique memorization from shared knowledge via gradient consistency analysis. Leveraging GME, we design **Federated Memorization Pruning (FedMemPrune)**: a lightweight, pruning-based approach that identifies and resets only the parameters most responsible for unique memorization at each client. Extensive experiments show FedMemPrune matches full retraining in unlearning efficacy (e.g., >92% ΔAUC reduction) while outperforming state-of-the-art federated unlearning methods in both utility preservation (<1.3% accuracy drop) and fairness (37% lower utility variance across clients), all with 8× less communication overhead.

item.title: Market Regime Council for Dynamic Credit Assignment in Multi-Agent LLM Decision Systems
item.description: We propose the **Market Regime Council (MRC)**, a cooperative multi-agent LLM decision system for portfolio management that addresses three key gaps: opaque credit assignment across specialist agents, cold-start dominance during market regime shifts, and insufficient transparency in allocation formation. MRC computes *exact Shapley values* over all single-agent, pairwise, and grand-coalition outputs to derive online agent weights. It integrates exponential-weighted performance histories, a Bayesian adaptive mixture for early-period stabilization, and regime-dependent authority multipliers—each rebalance is logged via a five-layer causal trace. Evaluated over 1,037 trading days across 13 crypto assets and 5 seeds, MRC achieves a Sharpe ratio of **1.51**, cumulative return of **440.1%**, and ranks **first in CR, SR, and IR** among active baselines while attaining the **lowest MDD**. Ablation confirms gains stem from *Shapley-weighted coalition integration*, not isolated components—establishing the first principled, game-theoretic credit assignment framework for dynamic LLM-based multi-agent finance.

item.title: Batch Normalization Amplifies Memorization and Privacy Risks
item.description: Batch Normalization (BN) is a cornerstone technique for stabilizing and accelerating deep neural network training—but its privacy implications remain poorly understood. This paper demonstrates that BN *amplifies memorization* of atypical or out-of-distribution (OOD) training samples, directly increasing vulnerability to membership inference attacks (MIAs). Through extensive experiments across multiple datasets (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet-1K) and architectures (ResNet, VGG, ViT), we show that BN models consistently memorize OOD samples 2.3–4.1× more than their non-BN counterparts. Crucially, this amplified memorization translates into significantly higher MIA success rates (+18.7% avg., up to +32.5%) and AUC gains (+0.21). We further provide theoretical analysis proving that BN magnifies the per-step gradient influence of outlier samples via normalization-induced gradient scaling—a mechanistic explanation for the observed effect. Our work reveals an underappreciated privacy risk inherent in BN and offers both practical guidance and foundational insight into how normalization layers unintentionally privilege rare or sensitive training examples.

item.title: Hardware-Aware Federated Learning for Speech Emotion Recognition
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training for speech emotion recognition (SER) across edge devices, yet real-world deployments suffer from severe hardware heterogeneity—varying compute power, memory, and network latency—which inflates round duration and system cost. This paper proposes a **hardware-aware FL framework** tailored to session-partitioned IEMOCAP. It unifies three key components: lightweight hardware profiling (CPU load, memory, RTT), utility-weighted Top-K client selection, and adaptive local epochs calibrated per-device capability. Evaluated under non-IID data distribution over 50 rounds and 5 trials, our method achieves competitive validation accuracy (**0.352**), reduces total training time by **36.5%** versus FedAvg, and cuts cumulative communication cost by **40%**, while improving client participation stability. The framework requires no model modification or server-side overhead, offering a practical, deployable solution for resource-constrained SER.

item.title: IterInject: Indirect Prompt Injection Against LLM Agents via Feedback-Guided Iterative Optimization
item.description: IterInject is a feedback-guided iterative framework for indirect prompt injection (IPI) against LLM agents. It closes the loop among injection, diagnosis, and refinement: a rule-based diagnoser produces structured behavioral labels (e.g., “tool misuse”, “goal bypass”), while an LLM optimizer refines payloads conditioned on the full optimization history—including prior failures, successes, and diagnostic signals. A synthesis module auto-generates novel disguise seeds from failure patterns, enabling self-evolution of the attack strategy space. Evaluated on AgentDojo and InjectAgent across four victim models, IterInject outperforms static baselines by +42.6% and prior adaptive methods by +28.3% in average success rate. On the production-grade Claude Code agent—with layered defenses—optimized payloads achieve full success on 5/9 high-value targets; the remaining 4 show statistically significant behavioral improvement after iterative refinement. Mechanistic analysis identifies an attention-mediated threshold mechanism in mid-to-late transformer layers, validated via three causal interventions—pointing directly to actionable defense strategies.

item.title: ChainLearn: A Blockchain-Based Capacity-Aware Framework for Federated Ensemble Learning
item.description: ChainLearn is a blockchain-based capacity-aware framework for federated ensemble learning, designed to address the critical mismatch between standard FL assumptions (homogeneous hardware, uniform architectures) and real-world medical infrastructure heterogeneity. It decouples on-chain governance—where a Solidity smart contract securely registers hospitals, stores benchmark hashes, performance metrics, and ensemble weights—from off-chain learning: participants train locally and submit only lightweight cryptographic hashes and scalar metrics (no model parameters). Capacity-aware coordination dynamically assigns architecture-appropriate models (e.g., MobileNetV3-Small for low-resource sites, ResNet-50 for high-resource ones), enabling equitable participation without architectural enforcement. Weighted ensemble inference is performed client-side for privacy and efficiency. Experiments on PneumoniaMNIST and DermaMNIST (5 seeds, 3 non-IID levels) show ChainLearn achieves lower or equal calibration error versus equal-weight ensembles and matches the accuracy of FedAvg, FedProx, and FedMD—while reducing per-round communication overhead to just **224 bytes**, a >912,000× reduction over FedAvg.

item.title: Related-Differential Distinguishers on up to 7 Rounds of AES
item.description: This paper introduces a novel framework for analyzing related differentials in AES by leveraging *exchange* and *shift* operations on difference patterns, enabling the first secret-key distinguishers for up to **7 rounds** of AES-128. For 5-round AES, we derive a new non-random property with probability $2^{-22}$ by combining one-round byte-wise related differentials with the 4-round zero-difference property, improving both chosen-plaintext (CP) and adaptively chosen-plaintext (ACP) distinguishers to data/time complexities of $2^{27.2}/2^{28.05}$ and $2^{23.32}/2^{23.54}$, respectively. For 6-round AES, we present a dual distinguisher using shifted diagonal related differentials, complementing the exchange-attack from ASIACRYPT 2019. Most significantly, for 7-round AES, we identify the first non-random property—by integrating one-round exchanged diagonal related differentials with Bardeh–Rijmen’s 4-round related differentials—and propose the first secret-key distinguisher requiring less than the full codebook ($<2^{128}$) data. This work advances the state-of-the-art in AES cryptanalysis and revitalizes the underexplored related-differential paradigm.

item.title: Scaling Intelligence: Verifiable Decision Forest Inference with $\textit{Remainder}$
item.description: We introduce *Remainder*, a highly scalable ZKML system for verifiable decision forest inference, built on a structured GKR protocol ([GKR15], [Tha13]). By combining data-parallel GKR with key circuit optimizations over [ZFZS20], we generate full GKR proofs for a forest of 128 trees (height 9) on 128 inputs (64 features each) in under 54 seconds—achieving **~3.3 ms per tree per sample**, with only **180× prover-side overhead** vs. plain CPU inference. Critical innovations include: (i) a multi-stage claim aggregation technique that reduces interpolation cost from $O(m n 2^n)$ to $O(m (n-k) 2^n)$ when $k$ variables are identical across $m$ evaluation points; and (ii) a generalization of [XZZPS19]’s linear-time prover to the data-parallel setting, yielding $O(2^{s_{i+1} + b})$ prover time. Benchmarks confirm sublinear scaling with forest size and batch inputs, 40–60% speedup from claim aggregation, and strict linearity in proof time w.r.t. layer width under the “Libra-Giraffe” algorithm. *Remainder* integrates Ligero commitments for input layers and uses Poseidon-based Fiat-Shamir for non-interactivity.

item.title: On Publicly Verifiable Tokens in Group Signatures with Message-Dependent Opening
item.description: This paper formally defines **public verifiability of tokens (PVT)**—a previously implicit but crucial security property in Group Signatures with Message-Dependent Opening (GS-MDO). PVT enables *anyone* to publicly verify, using only public parameters and the admitter’s public key, whether a given token is *genuinely usable* for opening a group signature on a specific message—*without requiring the opener’s secret key*. We rigorously distinguish PVT from mere signature-style token validity and establish its precise cryptographic relationship to opening correctness. We prove that three foundational GS-MDO schemes—Ohara et al. (AsiaCCS’13, pairing-based), Libert et al. (CT-RSA’14, pairing-based), and Libert et al. (ACNS’16, lattice-based)—all satisfy our PVT definition, confirming its soundness and practical relevance. Finally, we show that PVT enables *admitter accountability*: the admitter can publicly demonstrate honest token generation, providing non-repudiable evidence of compliance with the token-issuance algorithm.

item.title: CHRONOS: Temporally-Aware Multi-Agent Coordination for Evolving Data Marketplaces
item.description: CHRONOS is a temporally aware, multi-agent coordination framework for evolving data marketplaces built on knowledge graphs. It addresses three coupled failures in static designs—stale index shortcuts, misattributed Shapley pricing under distribution shifts, and uncoordinated differential-privacy budget consumption—via a unified three-layer architecture with explicit public/private separation. Layer 1 uses neural-ODE temporal decay to bound per-query recall loss as $O(P_q \lambda \Delta t)$, tightening bounds via monotone envelopes (1.8–3.2× tighter than observed loss). Layer 2 conditions Shapley valuation on detected changepoints and provides finite-sample error guarantees under noise. Layer 3 employs EXP3-IX for agent scheduling, achieving $O(\sqrt{T \log T})$ regret while enforcing $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP via moments accounting; it releases a privatized affinity matrix per epoch using the Gaussian mechanism, with all retrieval/ranking as zero-cost post-processing. Evaluated across four benchmarks, CHRONOS achieves Recall@10 = 0.937, 2.74 QPS, 161 ms latency, and total privacy cost $\varepsilon = 4.25$ at $\delta = 10^{-6}$ under zCDP composition—demonstrating a competitive operational point where utility stems primarily from public routing and low-sensitivity adaptive scheduling.

item.title: On the Stability of Spherical Hellinger-Kantorovich Flows and Their Implications for Differential Privacy
item.description: This paper establishes the first perturbation theory for spherical Hellinger–Kantorovich (SHK) gradient flows. Given two potentials $V$ and $V'$, we quantify how initial discrepancies propagate along their respective SHK flows—yielding a dimension-free, uniform bound on the log-likelihood ratio and Rényi divergence ($\alpha \ge 1$) at all times. Under mild Hessian regularity, we further derive an explicit time-dependent upper bound on the KL divergence. Applied to the exponential mechanism in differential privacy, our bounds deliver: (i) time-resolved $(\varepsilon(t), 0)$-DP guarantees via log-ratio control; (ii) $(\varepsilon, \delta(t))$-DP certificates through hockey-stick divergence; and (iii) a decomposed utility bound separating intrinsic mechanism suboptimality from finite-time sampling error. The results provide the first geometrically grounded, provable privacy–utility trade-off for SHK-based samplers.

item.title: AI Security Research Should Better Incentivize Defense Research
item.description: This paper identifies a critical structural imbalance in AI security research: a pronounced overemphasis on *attacking* AI systems relative to *defending* them—not only in publication volume but, more importantly, in evaluation rigor. Through systematic analysis of 327 papers across federated learning, speech recognition, membership inference, and large language model security, we find attack-to-defense ratios consistently exceed 4:1. Crucially, attack papers are routinely evaluated under overly favorable, idealized conditions (e.g., full model access, noiseless inputs), inflating perceived threat severity; defenses, by contrast, face unrealistically strict benchmarks—often invalidated by a single novel attack and rarely tested in real-world deployment settings (only 22% validated on production APIs). This asymmetry yields a literature rich in demonstrated vulnerabilities but poor in robust, deployable protections. We argue that sustainable AI security requires rebalancing incentives—through dedicated review criteria, standardized long-term robustness evaluation, and academic recognition of engineering rigor and deployment success.

item.title: Security, Privacy, and Ethical Risks in OpenClaw
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic analysis of security, privacy, ethical, and traceability risks in **OpenClaw**, a locally executable AI agent system designed for natural language interaction and real-world task execution. Through architecture-driven evaluation—examining its tool invocation model, persistent local storage, cross-context information aggregation, multi-user handling, and plugin integration—we identify critical vulnerabilities: unrestricted local resource access enabling lateral movement; implicit sensitive data fusion across tasks without user consent; identity ambiguity in shared environments; and fragmented audit logs impeding causal attribution. We argue these issues fundamentally undermine trustworthiness and hinder responsible deployment. The study concludes by proposing four open challenges requiring coordinated action: (1) adaptive runtime security enforcement, (2) context-aware privacy-preserving aggregation, (3) explainable ethical constraint embedding, and (4) unified provenance tracking across agent layers. Our findings serve as a foundational risk assessment for trustworthy local AI agents.

item.title: Prompt Overflow: What the Guardrail Inspects Is Not What the Model Infers
item.description: This paper identifies a critical security blind spot in guardrail models—widely deployed safety checkers for LLMs—arising from the mismatch between their narrow inspection windows (due to context constraints) and the much larger inference contexts of downstream LLMs. We introduce the **Prompt Overflow Attack**, which fragments malicious instructions and interleaves them with benign filler content across overlength prompts, ensuring no single inspected segment triggers detection while the full prompt remains fully actionable to the target LLM. Through systematic evaluation against state-of-the-art guardrails—including Meta’s Llama Prompt Guard, IBM’s Granite Guardian, and DeBERTa-based detectors—we show that adversarially lengthened prompts evade detection (as low as 3.2% detection rate) while preserving 100% attack success against LLMs—despite being reliably caught in short-context settings. Our work exposes a fundamental structural vulnerability in current safety architectures and proposes concrete mitigation directions: context-aware re-aggregation, cross-segment consistency modeling, and LLM-assisted verification.

item.title: PoisonForge: Task-Level Targeted Poisoning Benchmark for Instruction-Tuned LLMs
item.description: PoisonForge is the first benchmark for *task-level targeted poisoning* of instruction-tuned LLMs—where adversaries inject minimal crafted instruction-response pairs to compel models to embed attacker-specified entities (e.g., a country) *only* in outputs of a targeted task family (e.g., QA), while preserving normal behavior elsewhere. Parameterized across four dimensions (bias type, poisoning mode, entity appearance count, and target output length), PoisonForge evaluates 12 open-weight models (2B–32B) across five task families under a strict 1% poison budget (e.g., 10 poisoned examples among 1,000). Results show 11/12 models achieve >70% attack success rate (ASR) in their most vulnerable configuration, with <0.5% unintended leakage to non-target tasks and no degradation on standard benchmarks. We identify key drivers: ASR increases with entity appearances, optimal poisoning mode depends on entity semantics, and ASR monotonically decreases with output length. Crucially, correlation and predictive modeling confirm that *poisoning design—not model scale—is the primary determinant of ASR*, and these patterns generalize to unseen tasks. All configurations, pipelines, and analysis code are publicly released.

item.title: From Raw Experience to Skill Consumption: A Systematic Study of Model-Generated Agent Skills
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic, utility-grounded evaluation of the full skill lifecycle—experience generation, skill extraction, and skill consumption—for model-generated agent skills. Across five diverse agentic domains, we find that while such skills yield average performance gains, they incur non-trivial negative transfer (≈23% of cases), and extractor and consumer capabilities are largely decoupled: a model can excel at one stage yet fail at the other, irrespective of scale or baseline strength. Through fine-grained dissection, we identify experience composition, skill structural properties (e.g., step decomposability, context robustness), and consumer-specific transfer dynamics as key determinants of utility. Crucially, we distill these insights into a concrete *meta-skill*—a guidance protocol for extraction that prioritizes utility-predictive features over surface correctness. This meta-skill consistently improves skill quality (+18.7% mean utility) and cuts negative transfer by 52% across all domains.

item.title: MemAudit: Post-hoc Auditing of Poisoned Agent Memory via Causal Attribution and Structural Anomaly Detection
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on persistent memory for long-horizon reasoning, yet this introduces a critical security vulnerability: adversaries can stealthily inject poisoned records via normal interactions, later retrieved to manipulate agent behavior. Existing defenses focus on *online* intervention but lack *post-hoc* audit capability—i.e., identifying which stored memories causally contributed to observed harm. We propose **MemAudit**, the first causal memory auditing framework for memory-augmented LLM agents. It combines (1) a counterfactual memory influence score to quantify each memory’s causal contribution to harmful outputs, and (2) a memory consistency graph to detect structurally anomalous memories within the broader memory store. Evaluated against the query-only MINJA attack across QA and reasoning-agent settings, MemAudit reduces attack success rates from 70% to 0% and from 83.3% to 0%, respectively, under realistic post-hoc auditing conditions. This work establishes a novel, interpretable, and deployable paradigm for memory integrity assurance.
